The **x-intercept** is the point where a line crosses the horizontal x-axis. The key feature of *any* point on the x-axis is that its y-coordinate is always **0**.

### The Method: Step-by-Step Solution

Question: Do you prefer to use the method of plotting points or the method using the intercepts to graph the equation $$y = \frac{2}{3} x - 2$$ ? Why?
o3 Answer:
Both methods will lead to the same straight–line graph, but for this particular equation I prefer the intercept (or “slope-intercept”) method, and here is why.

1. The equation is already solved for y (y = 2/3 x – 2).  
   • The y-intercept is read off immediately: when x = 0, y = –2, so the point (0, –2) is on the line.  
   • The slope m = 2/3 tells me that for every 3 units I move to the right, I move 2 units up. Starting at (0, –2), one quick rise-over-run step gives a second point: (3, 0). These two points are enough to draw the line.

2. No arithmetic is needed beyond what is already visible; I do not have to substitute several x-values, calculate y for each, and then plot three or four points to be safe. That saves time and avoids computation errors.

3. The intercept method highlights key features of the line (its slope and intercept), which are often what I want to emphasize or interpret.

If the equation were in a less convenient form (for example 2x + 3y = 6) or had inconvenient fractional intercepts, I might be equally happy plotting a small table of points. But because y = 2/3 x – 2 is already in slope-intercept form and its intercepts are easy integers, the intercept/slope method is faster and clearer for me.

     So the line crosses the y-axis at (0, –2).

       0 = (2/3)x – 2

Plot those two points—(0, –2) and (3, 0)—and draw the straight line through them.

  •  Here both intercepts are integers, so there’s no messy arithmetic or fraction-plotting.  
  •  If I ever needed a third check, I could simply use the slope 2/3 starting from one intercept (go 3 units right and 2 units up) to land on the other.

By contrast, the “choose several x’s → compute y’s” approach can involve more work—especially if you pick values that lead to fractional y’s or if you end up plotting more points than you really need.  

(If you ever have an equation whose intercepts are awkward fractions, you might switch to choosing nice x–values and computing their y’s; or you could always start at the y-intercept and use the slope to “step” out additional points.)
